text: Psychologist Jerome Bruner distinguished two modes of thought: paradigmatic reasoning, which seeks logical proof, and narrative reasoning, which seeks verisimilitude — the feeling of truth. Crucially, Bruner found that the narrative mode dominates our everyday attention. We process the world not as a set of facts to verify but as a story already in progress, scanning for events that fit the plot we expect. This anticipatory structure is efficient but costly: stimuli that fall outside our current story arc are literally harder to perceive, demanding more neural resources to register at all.
question: What did Bruner find about stimuli that fall outside our current narrative?

options.text: They activate the paradigmatic reasoning mode instead
They are stored in long-term memory more reliably than expected events
They are harder to perceive and demand more neural resources to register
They trigger an automatic switch from narrative to logical processing
